title: The psoas muscle and its connection to stored fear
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
You don't know your psoas. But it knows you. 

 I spent years trying to think my way out of fear. I read the books, did the therapy, said the affirmations...
htmlContent:
You don't know your psoas. But it knows you.

I spent years trying to think my way out of fear. I read the books, did the therapy, said the affirmations. And still, my body held patterns that no amount of talking could touch. Here's the thing. The psoas muscle runs from your lower spine to the top of your femur. It's the deepest muscle in the human body. And it's directly wired into your survival brain.

When I first learned about the psoas, I was sitting in a workshop with a trauma-informed bodyworker. She asked us to place a hand on our lower belly and breathe. My psoas tightened like a fist. I felt a tremor run through my left hip. That was the moment I understood that fear isn't just in your head. It's in your tissues.

Think about it. Every time you've been startled, frightened, or threatened, your psoas contracted. It's the muscle that helps you curl into a fetal position. It's the muscle that prepares you to run. It's the muscle that freezes when running isn't an option. And if you've lived through chronic stress or trauma, your psoas might have been contracted for years. Decades. Maybe your whole life.

I'm not saying this to scare you. I'm saying this because I want you to know that what you're feeling is real. That tension in your hips, that ache in your lower back, that sense of being braced against the world. It's not weakness. It's your body doing exactly what it was designed to do. It's your body trying to keep you safe.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No. It helped me understand that the psoas isn't just a muscle. It's a storage unit for unprocessed experience.

What the researchers actually found

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed polyvagal theory, showed that the nervous system constantly scans for safety and danger. This happens below conscious awareness. Your psoas is part of that scanning system. When your vagus nerve detects threat, it signals the psoas to contract. When it detects safety, the psoas can relax.

Here's something wild. Your body can hold a trauma response for decades without you knowing it. You might think you've "dealt with" something because you can talk about it without crying. But talking about it uses the left hemisphere - language, logic, narrative. The trauma might still be living in your right hemisphere, in your body, in your implicit memory.

Pat Ogden's work on sensorimotor psychotherapy addresses exactly this gap. She noticed that clients could have years of talk therapy and still be triggered by the same things. Because the body hadn't been included in the conversation. The body was still bracing, still guarding, still waiting for the other shoe to drop.

Try this. Stand up for a moment if you can. Feel your feet on the ground. Now push gently against a wall with both hands. Feel the resistance. Feel your own strength pushing back. That's your body remembering that it can take action. That it's not helpless. That it has agency. Sometimes the body needs to complete a movement it was never allowed to make. That's not weird. That's healing.

But here's the problem. If you grew up in an environment where safety was rare or inconsistent, your nervous system learned to keep the psoas partially contracted all the time. It became a default state. Not a full contraction, not a full release. A chronic low-grade tension that you stopped noticing because it was always there.

Peter Levine, the developer of Somatic Experiencing, called this the "freeze response" that gets trapped in the body. He found that animals in the wild literally shake off the effects of threat after a close call. But humans override this process. We hold still. We stay polite. We keep going. And the energy of that uncompleted survival response gets stored in muscles like the psoas.

I remember the first time I felt my psoas release during a session. I was lying on a mat, and the practitioner gently placed her hand under my sacrum. I started trembling. Not from cold. From something older. A fear I'd been carrying since childhood. It lasted maybe two minutes. When it was over, I felt lighter. Like something had been lifted off my skeleton.

Wild, right? That a muscle can hold memories that your conscious mind has forgotten. But that's exactly what the research shows. The body keeps the score. And the psoas keeps the deepest part of that score.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score. It walks through the science of how trauma lives in the body, including the psoas, and what you can actually do about it.

How fear gets woven into your fascia

The psoas is wrapped in fascia, that web of connective tissue that runs through your entire body. Fascia is not passive. It's alive with nerve endings. It communicates with your autonomic nervous system constantly. When your psoas stays tight for years, the fascia around it thickens and shortens. It becomes rigid. It loses its glide.

This is why you can stretch your hips for months and feel no lasting change. The psoas isn't just tight. It's locked into a survival pattern. Stretching alone can't unwind that. You need to address the nervous system component.

Deb Dana, a clinician who works with polyvagal theory, talks about "glimmers" of safety. Small moments that signal to your nervous system that it's okay to relax. For the psoas to release, your brain needs to feel safe enough to let it go. That safety can't be faked. It has to be real.

Something happens in the body when safety has been absent for a long time. The muscles forget how to relax. Not metaphorically - literally. The fascia tightens. The diaphragm restricts. The psoas - that deep hip flexor that connects your spine to your legs - stays chronically contracted, ready to run or curl into a ball at any moment.

Bessel van der Kolk's research on yoga and trauma showed something worth noticing. Traumatized people who practiced yoga showed changes in their brain scans - specifically in the insula, the part of the brain responsible for interoception, for feeling what's happening inside your own body. Many trauma survivors have learned to disconnect from body sensation entirely. It was too dangerous to feel. Yoga - gentle, trauma-informed yoga - helped them come back.

I'm not saying yoga fixes everything. I'm saying the body needs to be included in healing. You can't think your way out of something that lives below thought. You have to feel your way through it. Slowly. With support. At your own pace. There's no rushing this.

I've found that slow, supported movements work better than aggressive stretching. Lying on your back with your knees bent and letting your legs fall to one side. Rolling your hips in gentle circles while standing. Lying over a bolster or a rolled blanket. These aren't exercises. They're invitations. You're asking your psoas if it's willing to soften. You're not demanding it.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored. It offers specific practices for finding safety in your body, which is exactly what your psoas needs to release stored fear.

The relationship between the psoas and your breath

Your psoas connects to your diaphragm through a shared fascial line. When your psoas is tight, your diaphragm can't move freely. Your breath becomes shallow. And when your breath is shallow, your nervous system gets more signals that something is wrong. It's a feedback loop. Fear tightens the psoas, which restricts the breath, which increases fear, which tightens the psoas more.

I spent years doing breathing exercises that told me to "breathe into my belly." But my belly wouldn't move. My psoas was holding it in place. No amount of conscious breathing could override a muscle that had been contracted for decades. I had to work with the psoas directly before my breath could change.

Here's what helped me. I started lying on my back with my knees bent and feet flat on the floor. I placed one hand on my lower belly and one hand on my chest. I didn't try to change my breath. I just watched it. I noticed that my belly barely moved. I noticed that my chest did most of the work. I noticed that my psoas felt hard, like a rope under my skin.

Then I started making small sounds on the exhale. A sigh. A hum. A long "ahhh." These sounds activate the vagus nerve, which sends a signal of safety down to the psoas. Over weeks, my belly started to soften. My breath started to drop lower. My psoas started to let go, millimeter by millimeter.

Notice what just happened in your body as you read that. Did your breath change? Did you feel a shift in your hips or lower back? That's your psoas responding to the possibility of safety. It's listening. It's always listening.

Look, I know this is heavy material. And I know there's a part of you that might be thinking "this is too much" or "I can't handle this" or "maybe I should just close this tab." That part is trying to protect you. And that's okay. But I want you to know that you can handle this. You've already handled worse. The fact that you're here, reading this, means your survival system did its job. You made it.

What we're doing here isn't about reliving the past. It's about understanding it well enough to stop being controlled by it. There's a difference between being trapped in a memory and looking at it from a safe distance. You get to choose how close you get. You get to choose when to step back. That choice - that agency - is itself a form of healing.

A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs. The gentle pressure can help signal safety to your psoas, especially when you're lying down and working with your breath.

What release actually feels like

I want to be honest with you. Releasing the psoas is not always pleasant. Sometimes it feels like sadness. Sometimes it feels like anger. Sometimes it feels like a wave of heat or a deep trembling. Your body has been holding these sensations for a long time. When they finally start to move, they can be intense.

That's okay. That's normal. That's your body processing what it couldn't process before.

I remember a session where my psoas released and I started sobbing. Not crying. Sobbing. A sound I didn't recognize coming from my own throat. It lasted maybe five minutes. When it was over, I felt a quietness in my hips that I had never felt before. Like something had been unlocked. Like a door had been opened that I didn't even know was closed.

Your experience might be different. You might feel nothing at first. You might feel a subtle warmth. You might feel a sense of expansion. There's no right way to do this. The only wrong way is to force it.

Sit with that. The psoas doesn't respond to force. It responds to patience, to presence, to permission. You can't bully your body into safety. You can only offer it the conditions to find its own way there.

If you're working with a therapist or bodyworker, tell them about your psoas. Ask them if they have experience with somatic approaches. If you're working on your own, go slow. Really slow. One minute of lying with your knees bent is enough. One gentle hip circle is enough. You don't have to fix everything today. You just have to start.

Healing Journey Resources

  • When the Body Says No - Connects the dots between stress and illness
  • The Body Keeps the Score - The science of how trauma lives in the body
  • Anchored - Practical nervous system practices for finding safety
  • YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs - Gentle pressure for calming the nervous system
